---
title: "Machine Modeling using Random Forest for current measles outbreaks in the U.S."
output: html_document
---
# Load Necessary Libraries and Set Seed
```{r}
#Ensure packages are installed if necessary
#Load necessary libraries
library(dplyr)
library(purrr)
library(lubridate)
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyverse)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(gtsummary)
library(GGally)
library(glmnet)
library(ranger)
library(tidymodels)
library(yardstick)
library(gt)
```

# Load the Cleaned Data
```{r}
#Load the cleaned data
measlesfeatures_clean <- readRDS("data/processed-data/measlesstatefeatures_data.rds")

#Select Variables of Interest for Machine Learning
ml_measles <- measlesfeatures_clean %>%
    select("total_cases_2024_26", "max_cases_80_09", "hsgradratediff", "mmr_mean", "frac_years_below_95")

reduced_data <- ml_measles %>%
    select("total_cases_2024_26", "max_cases_80_09", "hsgradratediff")

strong_data <- ml_measles %>%
    select("total_cases_2024_26", "mmr_mean", "frac_years_below_95")

#Develop a pairwise correlation for the continuous variables in this dataset
##Select the continuous variables
continuous_vars <- ml_measles %>%
  select(where(is.numeric))

#Make the correlation plot
ggpairs(continuous_vars)
```

```{r}
#Create an object while will run a pipeline that completes a random forest machine model, tunes the random forest model, cross-validates and fits the best RF model to the data based on Mean Absolute Error. 
run_rf_model <- function(data) {
  
  set.seed(1234)
  
  split <- initial_split(data, prop = 0.8)
  train <- training(split)
  test  <- testing(split)

  recipe_obj <- recipe(total_cases_2024_26 ~ ., data = train) %>%
    step_impute_median(all_numeric_predictors()) #Create a recipe for the total 2024-26 measles cases
  
  model <- rand_forest(
    trees = 500,
    mtry  = tune(),
    min_n = tune()
  ) %>%
    set_engine("ranger", importance = "permutation") %>%
    set_mode("regression")#Random Forest Model
  
  wf <- workflow() %>%
    add_model(model) %>%
    add_recipe(recipe_obj)#Create workflow using the random forest model and the recipe
  
  folds <- vfold_cv(train, v = 5)#Cross-validate using folds
  
  grid <- grid_regular(
    mtry(range = c(1, max(1, ncol(train) - 1))),
    min_n(range = c(2, 20)),
    levels = 5
  )#Create a grid to tune off of for the tune_grid() step
  
  tune_res <- tune_grid(
    wf,
    resamples = folds,
    grid = grid,
    metrics = metric_set(mae)
  )#Tune the random forest model using the grid with an output metric of Mean Absolute Error
  
  best <- select_best(tune_res, metric = "mae")#Select the model with the best performing MAE
  
  final_wf <- finalize_workflow(wf, best)#Finalize the workflow
  
  final_fit <- fit(final_wf, data = train)#Fit the data to the final workflow
  
  preds <- predict(final_fit, test) %>%
    bind_cols(test)#Generate predictions on the testing data
  
  metrics <- metrics(preds,
                      truth = total_cases_2024_26,
                      estimate = .pred)#Compare the predictions to the original total_cases_2024_26 data
  
  tibble(
    mae = metrics %>% filter(.metric == "mae") %>% pull(.estimate)
  )#Extract the MAE metrics and place into a tibble
}

#Generate Linear Model to Compare the Random Forest Model to

run_lm_model <- function(data) {
  
  set.seed(1234)
  
  split <- initial_split(data, prop = 0.8)
  train <- training(split)
  test  <- testing(split)
  
  recipe_obj <- recipe(total_cases_2024_26 ~ ., data = train) %>%
    step_impute_median(all_numeric_predictors())
  
  model <- linear_reg() %>%
    set_engine("lm") %>%
    set_mode("regression")
  
  wf <- workflow() %>%
    add_model(model) %>%
    add_recipe(recipe_obj)
  
  fit_model <- fit(wf, data = train)
  
  preds <- predict(fit_model, test) %>%
    bind_cols(test)
  
  metrics <- metrics(preds,
                     truth = total_cases_2024_26,
                     estimate = .pred)
  
  tibble(
    mae = metrics %>% filter(.metric == "mae") %>% pull(.estimate)
  )
}

#Run the model workflow designed above on the three different datasets: Full contains all variables, Reduced contains variabled we predict are having little predictive power, and strong-only includes variables we believe might have some predictive power
results <- tibble(
  dataset = c("Full", "Reduced", "Strong-only"),
  data    = list(ml_measles, reduced_data, strong_data)
) %>%
  mutate(
    rf_metrics = map(data, run_rf_model),
    lm_metrics = map(data, run_lm_model)
  ) %>%
  unnest(cols = c(rf_metrics, lm_metrics), names_sep = "_") %>%
  rename(
    rf_mae = rf_metrics_mae,
    lm_mae = lm_metrics_mae
  )

#Compare the MAE across the different variables used as predictors of total_cases_2024_26
results <- results %>%
  mutate(
    mae_difference = lm_mae - rf_mae
  )

results

#Re-name dataset titles for cleaner display in output table
results <- results %>%
  mutate(
    dataset = recode(dataset,
      "Full" = "All 4 Predictor Variables",
      "Reduced" = "Historical Burdens + High School Graduation Rates",
      "Strong-only" = "MMR Vaccination Mean + Fraction of Years Below 95% Vaccination"
    )
  )

#Create a table to display the results of the linear and random forest modeling
results %>%
  select(dataset, rf_mae, lm_mae, mae_difference) %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), round, 2)) %>%
  gt() %>%
  tab_header(
    title = "Model Comparison: Random Forest vs Linear Regression",
    subtitle = "Mean Absolute Error (MAE) Across Three Predictor Variable Combinations"
  ) %>%
  cols_label(
    dataset = "Dataset",
    rf_mae = "RF MAE",
    lm_mae = "LM MAE",
    mae_difference = "LM - RF"
  ) %>%
  fmt_number(
    columns = c(rf_mae, lm_mae, mae_difference),
    decimals = 2
  ) %>%
  cols_align(
    align = "center",
    -dataset
  ) %>%
    tab_source_note(
    source_note = "All 4 predictor variables include the combination of Historical Burdens, High School Graduation Rates, MMR Vaccination Means and Fraction of Years Below 95% Vacciantion. Historical Burdens is the maximum measles case burden between 1980 and 2009. High School Graduation Rates is the difference in high school graduation rates over time between 2012 and 2022. MMR Vaccination Mean is the mean MMR vaccination rate amongst kindergarteners from 2012 to 2024. Fraction of Years Below 95% Vaccination is a measure of the years a state was below the target goal of 95% MMR vaccination coverage from 2012 to 2024."
  ) %>%
tab_style(
  style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
  locations = list(
    cells_column_labels(everything()),
    cells_title(groups = c("title", "subtitle"))
  )) %>%
data_color(
  columns = c(rf_mae, lm_mae),
  colors = scales::col_numeric(
    palette = c("lightgreen", "white"),
    domain = NULL
  )
) %>%
  gtsave("RandomForestMeasles.png")
```
